Lab Guide › Working in the Lab

Lab operations roles

How we share lightweight ownership of the operational tasks that keep the lab running.

Getting Started

  • Start guide: Undergraduates
  • Start guide: Graduate students
  • Start guide: Postdocs

  • Time off & vacation
  • Support resources

  • Pay for things
  • Travel
  • Ship packages
  • Lab operations roles

  • Write a first draft
  • Design your figures

Professional Development

  • Attending a conference
  • Applying for funding
  • Request recommendation letters
  • File your dissertation

Purpose. Keep the lab running smoothly with clear, lightweight ownership of essential operational tasks — so safety, facilities, equipment, data, and onboarding don’t fall through the cracks, and we can stay focused on science.

Cadence. Weekly (2–5 min): owners review assigned tasks in the task tracker. Monthly (10–15 min): owners update checklists and flag overdue items. Quarterly (30–60 min): owners complete reviews, walkthroughs, and compliance checks. Annually (4–6 hours): owners propose process improvements. Most roles average ~10–30 min/week.

Escalation. If something is blocked for over a week — or immediately if it’s safety- or compliance-critical — the owner reaches out to Christina with what happened, what you need, and by when.

No role should require chasing individuals. If someone doesn’t comply twice, or you find yourself chasing external folks, escalate to Christina.

The roles below rotate among lab members. Each owner maintains a handoff page so the next person can run the role from that page alone.

The roles

Onboarding Officer — makes onboarding fast, consistent, and safe: onboarding checklists, initial-training coordination, access-request guidance, and starter templates. Can require completion of the onboarding checklist before independent work.

Safety Officer — keeps safety practices current and visible: walkthroughs, certification tracking, documentation, and critical-equipment checks. Can pause unsafe work and require refresher training before re-access.

Facilities & Infrastructure Coordinator — keeps spaces functional and reduces downtime: facilities tickets, infrastructure issues, and room readiness. Can submit facilities requests and set limited-access windows for repairs.

Equipment & Experiment Coordinator — keeps core equipment reliable and easy to use: maintenance schedules, calibration reminders, and SOP/lead-user coverage for each major system (wind tunnel, cameras, 3D printer). Can restrict equipment use until training/SOP requirements are met.

IT & Data Administrator — keeps code and data organized, backed up, and reproducible: GitHub norms, data-storage structure, backups, permissions, and license tracking. Can require a minimum repo/data structure and lock down permissions if integrity is at risk.

Communication & Outreach Lead — keeps the lab’s outward-facing presence legible and attractive to collaborators, recruits, and funders: website updates, social media, tour scripts, and outreach events. Can publish routine updates and set a monthly content schedule.

Animal Care Coordinator — ensures animal welfare and compliance are handled predictably: feeding/monitoring schedules, welfare logs, IACUC reminders, and Raptor Center coordination. Can pause animal-related activities if welfare or compliance is at risk.

Inventory & Process Improvement Lead — keeps common consumables stocked and launches small workflow upgrades: inventory tracking, reorder thresholds, and the annual chemical-inventory review. Can reorder consumables under an agreed threshold.

Offboarding & Access Control Officer — makes departures smooth and low-risk: offboarding checklists, access removal, ownership transfer, and the quarterly access audit. Can freeze or remove access to lab systems after a member’s last day.
